svg.g: <rect width="300" height="50" fill="#F7F7F7" rx="10" data-narration="The Principle of Execution Monitoring is simple: run the malware and watch what it does. However, doing this safely and capturing all relevant actions requires specialized tools. These tools typically operate by intercepting system calls (APIs) made by the malicious process."]

<rect width="300" height="50" fill="#F7F7F7" rx="10" data-narration="Monitoring tools use techniques like in-line hooking and SSDT hooking to intercept system calls. In-line hooking modifies the first few instructions of an API function in memory to jump to the analyst's code, which logs the call and its parameters before executing the original function. SSDT hooking modifies the System Service Descriptor Table, which is a kernel structure that holds pointers to system call functions. This is a more advanced and powerful technique, often used by rootkits and thus a key area to monitor for malicious activity."]

<rect width="300" height="50" fill="#F7F7F7" rx="10" data-narration="Behavior monitoring is never performed on a live product. Instead, it's done in a controlled environment, such as a sandbox. This allows analysts to capture the runtime behavior of malicious software without causing harm to the system or its users."]
